Why this helps: the hero gets high priority while below-the-fold assets lazy-load. Explicit width/height prevents CLS, and srcset/sizes avoids over-downloading on small screens.

Common pitfalls
  • Lazy-loading above-the-fold hero media, which can worsen LCP.
  • Optimizing one metric in isolation (for example, better LCP but worse INP due to heavy hydration).
  • Skipping real-user monitoring and trusting only synthetic lab results.

rows: What broad opportunities exist on a cold page load?
Spot large images, blocking scripts, and cache misses.
What exactly blocked render or created long tasks?
Trace CSS/script blockers, bundle cost, and hydration work.
Did real users actually improve after release?
Compare p75 LCP/INP by device and network before shipping the next fix.
blocks.caption: Use one measurement loop instead of guessing.

blocks.text: Worked scenario: first-visit fix vs repeat-visit reality A cold-load waterfall shows 220 KB of render-blocking CSS, an oversized hero image, and a delayed application bundle. You inline critical CSS and ship a smaller hero, so LCP improves. On repeat visits, immutable asset caching removes the JS/CSS re-download cost, but HTML and API responses still revalidate and hydration leaves a long task that hurts INP. That is why 'LCP got better' does not mean the page is fully fixed.

blocks.code:
# Versioned assets used on repeat visits
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable

# HTML or mutable API shell that must stay fresh
Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate

blocks.text: When to use / when not to use Use quick wins (compression, cache headers, image sizing) first when pages are broadly slow and budgets are unclear. Use deeper changes (route-level code splitting, partial hydration, worker offload) when profiling shows persistent long tasks or framework overhead. Do not start with exotic micro-optimizations before you have a baseline and top bottleneck.

Interview follow-ups Q1: Which metric would you prioritize for an ecommerce landing page? A: Start with LCP for conversion-sensitive first impressions, then INP for interaction-heavy flows. Q2: How do you prove improvement? A: Compare before/after p75 Core Web Vitals in production, segmented by device and network class. Q3: What if backend latency dominates? A: Improve cache hit ratio, edge rendering strategy, and payload size while coordinating API response budgets.
Field validation snippet Lab tools explain why a page is slow. Field data tells you whether real users actually improved after the release.

Practical scenario
A landing page improves hero-image delivery and removes one render blocker, so lab LCP gets better. Users on mid-tier phones still feel lag when the add-to-cart button hydrates because main-thread work and event wiring hurt INP.

What to do next
Keep the LCP fix, then profile hydration, long tasks, and bundle boundaries instead of assuming the page is now “fast enough.”

Implementation checklist / takeaway Set clear budgets, fix critical path first, add safe caching rules, then verify with RUM dashboards. Strong answers are metric-driven, layered, and explicit about trade-offs.

seo.title: Web load-time optimization: critical path, Core Web Vitals trade-offs, and RUM
seo.description: Explain load-time optimization through critical-path debugging, render-blocking work, caching, LCP-vs-INP trade-offs, and field validation with Core Web Vitals.

incidentCard.title: Load-Time Priority Check
incidentCard.scenario: A product page is slow on first load. One teammate wants to start with tiny code micro-optimizations in a helper function, while another points out a heavy hero image, render-blocking CSS/JS, and no effective caching. What is the better explanation?

options.label: Start with the biggest critical-path wins: reduce transfer size, unblock rendering, optimize key assets like the hero image, and set sensible caching. Small code-level tweaks matter later, but they are usually not the first fix for slow page loads.
Begin with the smallest JavaScript functions because runtime efficiency is almost always the main reason pages load slowly, even before network and rendering costs are investigated.

The main fix for slow loads is giving the server more CPU and memory. Frontend asset strategy, compression, and render-blocking resources usually have only minor impact compared with backend hardware.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Close. The important point is prioritization: page-load performance usually improves fastest by reducing network cost, removing render blockers, and optimizing critical assets before chasing low-impact micro-optimizations.

title: What is the difference between reflow and repaint in browsers?
description: Reflow (layout) recalculates element geometry, while repaint redraws visual styles without changing layout geometry. Strong answers also explain paint-heavy visuals, composite-only updates, and why forced reflow causes runtime jank.

blocks.text: The core idea Browsers render in stages. The two most asked stages are: - Reflow (layout): recalculate element size/position. - Repaint: redraw pixels (colors, shadows, visibility) without changing geometry. Reflow is usually more expensive because geometry changes can cascade through large parts of the DOM tree. Paint-only work is not free either when you redraw large areas or heavy visual effects, but it usually skips layout.

blocks.caption: Reflow can trigger repaint; repaint does not always require reflow.

blocks.text: Paint vs composite follow-up Paint-only changes can still be expensive when they redraw large backgrounds, shadows, filters, or blur effects. Composite-only updates, often with transform or opacity on isolated layers, are usually cheaper because the browser can move existing pixels instead of recalculating layout.

What makes reflow expensive Layout changes can invalidate measurements of nearby or ancestor elements. On large pages, one geometry change can force the browser to recalculate positions for many nodes. This becomes visible as scroll jank, delayed input response, and dropped frames during animations.

Forced synchronous reflow (layout thrashing) A common performance bug: write styles, then immediately read layout metrics, repeatedly in a loop. Reading layout properties after writes can force the browser to flush layout right away.

rows: Batch reads first, then batch writes in requestAnimationFrame
Prevents repeated forced reflow in loops.
Often avoids layout and heavy paint work.
Reduces how far layout invalidation spreads.
Toggle CSS classes or batch DOM updates (fragments/virtual DOM batching)
blocks.caption: Most fixes are about reducing unnecessary layout recalculation.

blocks.text: How to diagnose in DevTools Record a trace in the Performance panel while reproducing jank: - Look for frequent Layout events and long Paint tasks. - Correlate long frames with JavaScript that reads layout after style writes. - Check whether animation steps stay near the 16.7ms/frame budget (60fps target).

Interview one-liner Reflow recalculates layout geometry and is generally more expensive; repaint redraws visuals without layout changes. Optimize by minimizing layout invalidations, batching DOM reads/writes, and animating transform/opacity where possible.
Still so complicated? Think of reflow as moving furniture around a room (positions change), while repaint is changing wall color (same layout, new appearance). Moving furniture takes more work.

Practical scenario
A filter sidebar and analytics dashboard both update card dimensions on every keystroke, causing repeated layout reads and writes that create visible typing lag. Paint-heavy shadows on the cards make the slow frames even more obvious.

Common pitfalls
  • Reading getBoundingClientRect() right after multiple style mutations.
  • Animating top/left/width for high-frequency transitions.
  • Using large blur, shadow, or filter effects and assuming “it is only repaint”.
  • Ignoring trace data and guessing performance bottlenecks.
Trade-off or test tip
Measure before and after in DevTools and RUM. Compare frame times and INP when switching from layout-triggering animations to transform/opacity patterns.

seo.title: Reflow vs repaint: layout thrashing, paint cost, and composite debug
seo.description: Understand reflow vs repaint, where layout thrashing comes from, why paint-heavy visuals still hurt, and when transform/opacity can stay in cheaper composite paths.

incidentCard.title: Layout Jank Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate updates element widths in a loop and reads layout measurements right after each write. Typing in the UI starts to feel laggy, and they say, "It is probably just repaint work." What is the better explanation?

options.label: Repaint and reflow are effectively the same browser step, so if the UI feels slow the real issue is usually just too many colors, shadows, or gradients on the page.
Modern browsers automatically batch every DOM read and write, so doing measurement and style changes in the same loop should not create meaningful performance issues.

Changing geometry like width can trigger reflow, and mixing layout reads with writes can force repeated synchronous layout work. That kind of layout thrashing is usually more expensive than a simple repaint.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Close. The key point is that layout work is different from paint work. Geometry changes and forced layout reads can cause expensive reflow thrashing, so batching reads and writes matters.

title: How do you evaluate modern JavaScript trends for production frontend apps?
description: Interview-ready JavaScript trends answer focused on durable themes: TypeScript adoption, ESM-first packaging, performance budgets, runtime/tooling changes, and practical ways to stay current without hype.

blocks.text: Definition (above the fold) A strong JavaScript trends answer is not a list of shiny tools. It is a product-focused evaluation of durable shifts that change reliability, performance, and delivery speed in production frontend apps. Interviewers want to hear why the trend matters, what problem it solves, and how you would validate impact.
Core mental model Use this lens for each trend: trend -> problem solved -> metric improved -> trade-off introduced. If you cannot connect all four parts, it is probably hype, not an actionable trend.

columns: How to explain it in interviews
rows: TypeScript + schema validation reduce production regressions
Mention fewer runtime bugs and safer refactors
Packages now optimize for ESM, exports maps, and tree-shaking
Explain bundle size and dead-code elimination benefits
Teams track LCP/INP/CLS with budgets and alerts
Show metric-driven optimization instead of vague speed claims
SSR, streaming, and edge execution reduce TTFB and improve UX
Describe how rendering strategy follows route behavior
Modern dev servers, linting, and test pipelines shorten iteration loops
Tie faster feedback to fewer escaped defects
Teams use assistants but keep tests/reviews as quality gates
blocks.caption: Pick 3-4 themes and go deep on impact, not buzzwords.

blocks.text: Runnable example #1: ESM-first package shape
Why this matters: a clear ESM export map improves interoperability, and sideEffects: false helps bundlers tree-shake unused modules safely.
Runnable example #2: measure long tasks as part of performance trend tracking
This turns the "performance trend" into measurable production telemetry instead of a generic claim.

rows: Create a migration ticket with owner and deadline
Block release when budgets are exceeded
Adopt lint rules or tests that prevent recurrence
blocks.caption: How to stay current with a repeatable process.

blocks.text:
Common pitfalls
  • Listing tool names without explaining problem/impact/trade-offs.
  • Calling something a trend without evidence from production metrics.
  • Ignoring migration and maintenance cost when adopting new runtimes or frameworks.

Interview follow-ups Q1: Which trend would you prioritize first in a legacy app? A: Type safety and observability, because they reduce regression risk before deeper rewrites. Q2: How do you reject hype? A: Ask for a baseline metric, a target metric, and rollback criteria before adoption. Q3: How do you keep up without burnout? A: Time-box weekly review, automate alerts, and only deep-dive trends tied to current product goals.

Implementation checklist / takeaway Pick a few durable JavaScript trends, map each to measurable outcomes, and show explicit trade-offs. Strong interview answers sound like an engineering decision record, not a social media trend recap.

seo.title: What are current JavaScript trends in production apps?
seo.description: Current JavaScript trends include TypeScript adoption, native ESM workflows, faster tooling, and performance-focused rendering patterns.

incidentCard.title: Trend Evaluation Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate wants to adopt a new JavaScript tool because "everyone on social media is talking about it." There is no clear production problem, no target metric, and no rollback plan if it fails. What is the better explanation?

options.label: If enough teams are talking about a tool, that is usually enough evidence to adopt it quickly. Metrics and rollback plans matter more after the migration is already underway.
Modern JavaScript trends are almost always net improvements, so the main risk is falling behind. The best strategy is to upgrade first and validate business value later.

A production trend should be evaluated by the problem it solves, the metric it is expected to improve, and the trade-offs it introduces. If you cannot name those clearly, it is probably hype rather than an engineering decision.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Close. The key lesson is that good trend evaluation is evidence-based: connect the trend to a real problem, a measurable outcome, and explicit trade-offs before adopting it.

title: How should a front-end handle streaming data from an AI model?
description: This checks whether you can build a production streaming UI state machine: append partial output safely, preserve final message correctness, support cancellation, and recover from mid-stream failures.

blocks.text: The Core Idea Treat AI streaming as a production UI state machine, not just a transport detail. You open a stream (SSE, WebSocket, or fetch + ReadableStream), append partial output as chunks arrive, keep partial and final message state consistent, and stop cleanly when the user cancels or the stream fails.

rows: Connect via SSE/WebSocket or fetch stream
Update UI incrementally as data arrives
blocks.caption: A minimal streaming flow

blocks.text:
Production scenario
Suppose a chat answer streams for 20 seconds and the user switches prompts halfway through. If you do not model streaming, completed, cancelled, and failed states explicitly, you can show mixed output, mark a partial answer as final, or append late tokens into the wrong conversation. Good implementations treat partial text, final text, retry UI, and cancellation as separate states.

Summary Open a stream, append chunks to the UI, and handle cancel + errors gracefully. The UX should feel responsive even before the full response finishes, but the real production requirement is keeping partial and final output correct.

seo.title: AI streaming in frontend: partial output, cancellation, and failure handling
seo.description: Handle AI streaming as a production state machine: render chunks incrementally, preserve partial and final output correctness, support cancellation, and recover from mid-stream failures.

incidentCard.title: Streaming UI Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate waits for the full AI response before rendering anything because it feels simpler. Another teammate wants to append chunks as they arrive, let the user cancel, and show an error state if the stream breaks halfway through. What is the better explanation?

options.label: A streaming UI should render partial output incrementally, support cancellation, and handle mid-stream failures cleanly. That gives faster feedback and avoids trapping the user behind a long-running request.
Waiting for the full response is usually better because streaming adds visual noise. Cancellation and partial-state handling are unnecessary if the backend eventually returns the complete answer.
Incremental rendering is only useful with WebSockets. If the frontend uses fetch or SSE, it should treat the result like a normal full response and update the UI once at the end.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Close. The key idea is that streaming is a UX pattern, not just a transport detail. The frontend should show progress incrementally, manage partial state safely, and allow the user to stop or recover from failures.

title: How would you maintain conversation state in a chat app like ChatGPT?
description: Conversation state in chat apps is a production state-machine problem: stable message IDs, streaming draft buffers, retries, reconnects, truncation, and ordering guarantees all need explicit transitions.

blocks.text: Production state machine Conversation state in a chat app is not just an array of messages. It is a set of explicit transitions: user submit, optimistic render, stream append, finalize, retry, reconnect, and recovery. If those transitions are implicit, you get duplicate messages, stale drafts, and ordering bugs.

Core mental model Use a finite-state message lifecycle: pending -> streaming -> done | error. Keep canonical message entities separate from UI-only controls (composer text, active request id, autoscroll lock) so retries and reconnects are predictable.

blocks.caption: Separate canonical message state from UI helper state.

blocks.text: Runnable example #1: reducer transitions
This pattern avoids out-of-order updates by rejecting stale chunks that do not match the active request id.
Runnable example #2: retry + finalize path
Keep retry metadata on the failed assistant turn so the user can retry that exact turn without duplicating prior history.

Common pitfalls
  • Appending chunks without request IDs, causing duplicate or out-of-order assistant text.
  • Mutating optimistic messages in place and failing to reconcile when the server returns canonical IDs.
  • Keeping unbounded history in memory and payloads, which slows rendering and increases token cost.

When to use / when not to use Use local-only ephemeral state for short support chats or anonymous sessions. Use persisted server-backed state for cross-device continuity, analytics, moderation, and reliable retry history. Do not persist everything by default if privacy scope is unclear; define retention and redaction policies first.

Interview follow-ups Q1: How do you prevent stale chunks after a new prompt? A: Tag every stream with a requestId and discard chunks that do not match current activeStream. Q2: How do you keep scroll behavior usable? A: Auto-scroll only when user is near bottom; freeze auto-scroll when user reads history. Q3: How do you handle token limits? A: Maintain rolling summaries/checkpoints and send only recent turns plus summary context.

Implementation checklist / takeaway Define message state transitions first, enforce request-scoped streaming, then add persistence and truncation rules. Strong answers show reliability under retries, reconnects, and long sessions.

incidentCard.title: Conversation Timeline Check
incidentCard.scenario: A chat app starts showing duplicate assistant text after a user sends a second prompt before the first streamed reply finishes. One teammate wants to keep appending chunks to whichever assistant message is last in the array. Another wants each request to create its own assistant placeholder, track the active request ID, and ignore chunks from older streams. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Always append new chunks to the last assistant message in the array. As long as messages stay visually sorted, you do not need separate request tracking or message lifecycle states.
The real fix is to persist every message to the backend as soon as a chunk arrives. Once storage is reliable, duplicate text and out-of-order stream updates stop being a frontend state problem.

Treat chat history as an ordered timeline with stable message IDs, explicit statuses, and request-scoped streaming. Create an assistant placeholder per request and discard stale chunks that do not match the active request.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key idea is deterministic state transitions: pending, streaming, done, or error. Keep canonical message entities separate from UI helpers, tag streams with a request ID, and reject stale chunks instead of guessing which message to append to.

seo.title: Chat conversation state in production: streaming buffers, retries, and ordering bugs
seo.description: Explain chat state through the real pitfalls: append-only timelines, stable IDs, streaming drafts, retry or reconnect handling, and message-order bugs.

title: SSE vs WebSocket in JavaScript: which real-time transport should you choose?
description: Practical SSE vs WebSocket comparison for frontend interviews: protocol behavior, reconnect strategy, infrastructure trade-offs, and architecture decisions for AI streaming, notifications, chat, and collaboration.

blocks.text: Definition (above the fold) Use SSE (Server-Sent Events) when updates are mostly server -> client and you want a simple HTTP-based stream with automatic reconnect. Use WebSocket when you need true bidirectional, low-latency messaging (client and server both sending frequently). The choice should follow interaction shape, not preference.

Decision heuristic Ask two questions first: (1) Does the client need to push frequent live events upstream? (2) Do you need strict duplex semantics like collaborative cursors or multiplayer state sync? If both answers are no, SSE is often cheaper and simpler to operate.

blocks.caption: Transport choice matrix for frontend system design interviews.

blocks.text: Runnable example #1: SSE client with typed events
SSE keeps the client code compact for one-way streams and handles reconnect internally, which is ideal for feed-like updates.
Runnable example #2: WebSocket with heartbeat + reconnect

rows: One-way token delivery with simple reconnect
Server pushes events, client rarely sends state
Simple fan-out over existing HTTP infrastructure
blocks.caption: Choose transport by product behavior, not by trend.

blocks.text:
Common pitfalls
  • Choosing WebSocket by default when one-way streaming would be simpler with SSE.
  • Ignoring reconnection/backoff and duplicate message handling in WebSocket clients.
  • Skipping auth token refresh strategy for long-lived connections.

Interview follow-ups Q1: How do you handle message ordering on reconnect? A: Use monotonic event IDs and replay from last acknowledged ID. Q2: What about backpressure? A: Batch/coalesce updates on client and cap queue growth with drop or snapshot policies. Q3: Can you migrate from SSE to WebSocket later? A: Yes, keep a transport adapter boundary so UI logic remains protocol-agnostic.

Implementation checklist / one-sentence answer Use SSE for simple server-to-client streaming over HTTP; use WebSocket for true bidirectional, high-frequency interaction. Validate the choice with connection lifecycle, infra cost, and failure-recovery requirements.

incidentCard.title: Realtime Transport Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate wants to use WebSocket for an AI answer stream because 'real-time always means WebSocket.' Another teammate says the browser mostly just receives tokens from the server, so SSE is the better default unless the feature needs fast two-way messaging like collaborative cursors or multiplayer state sync. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Use WebSocket for any product that feels real-time. If the UI updates live, full-duplex transport is always the safer default, even when the client rarely sends anything upstream.
Either transport is basically interchangeable as long as you add reconnect logic. The important decision is styling the chat well, not whether the stream is one-way or bidirectional.

Use SSE when updates are mostly server to client, like AI token streams or notifications. It stays on normal HTTP, keeps the client simpler, and gives you built-in reconnect without full-duplex complexity.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The choice should follow interaction shape, not preference. SSE is usually the simpler fit for one-way server to client streams, while WebSocket earns its extra complexity when the browser and server both need frequent low-latency messaging.

seo.title: SSE vs WebSocket: which should you use?
seo.description: Use SSE for one-way server-to-client streaming, and use WebSocket for full-duplex real-time communication.

title: AI UX in Frontend Apps: handling latency, streaming, trust, and failures
description: Deep dive on AI UX design for frontend interviews: uncertainty handling, streaming states, trust and safety cues, retries, cancellation, and robust interaction patterns for LLM-powered features.

blocks.text: Definition (above the fold) AI UX is hard because model output is probabilistic, latency varies, and failures are common. A high-quality frontend makes this uncertainty visible and controllable: users must understand what is happening now, what could go wrong, and what they can do next.

Core product model Treat AI responses as a state machine, not a single API call: idle -> submitting -> streaming -> done | error | canceled. Separate canonical message state from transient UI state (typing indicator, partial tokens, retry banner) to avoid race conditions and duplicate output.

rows: Render incremental draft with clear streaming label
Input validation and suggested fixes before submit
blocks.caption: AI UX should be measurable with behavior and reliability metrics.

blocks.text: Runnable example #1: reducer for AI response lifecycle
This explicit lifecycle prevents stale updates and gives the UI deterministic rendering rules for each state.
Runnable example #2: cancellable streaming request with safe finalize

rows: Prevents wasted time and token cost
Fast retry for weak answer quality
Reuse last prompt + new request ID
Strip unsafe markup before clipboard write
Attach prompt, response, and error metadata
blocks.caption: Controls convert uncertainty into user agency.

blocks.text:
Common pitfalls
  • Showing a spinner with no progress signal during long generation.
  • Merging stream chunks without request IDs, causing mixed outputs from parallel prompts.
  • Using vague error messages (for example, "Something went wrong") without recovery actions.
  • Presenting generated text as guaranteed fact without confidence or source cues.

When to use / when not to use Use streaming + rich controls when response time is variable and output quality may require iteration. For deterministic workflows (for example, fixed schema extraction), simplify the UX and prioritize clear validation over conversational UI patterns.

Interview follow-ups Q1: How do you build trust when model answers can be wrong? A: Add confidence/source cues, easy correction flow, and transparent error handling. Q2: How do you prevent stale streaming from previous prompts? A: Track request IDs and ignore chunks that do not match the active request. Q3: Which metric would you track first? A: Time-to-first-token, stream completion rate, and retry success rate.

Implementation checklist / takeaway Model explicit AI states, stream progressively, expose user controls, and design for failure as a first-class path. Strong AI UX answers combine reliability, clarity, and trust rather than only visual polish.

incidentCard.title: AI UX Reliability Check
incidentCard.scenario: A product team ships an AI writing assistant that shows only a spinner while generating. If the request fails after 20 seconds, the UI replaces everything with 'Something went wrong.' Another teammate argues the experience should show progress, stream partial output, let the user stop or retry, and make uncertainty visible instead of pretending the answer is guaranteed. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Treat AI output as a lifecycle like submitting, streaming, done, error, or canceled. Show partial progress, keep retry and stop controls visible, and use trust cues so users understand what happened and what they can do next.
Keep the UX simple by hiding intermediate states. A single spinner and a generic error message are usually better because they avoid distracting the user with AI-specific uncertainty.
The main AI UX problem is visual polish. As long as the interface looks clean, streaming labels, cancellation, and recovery controls are secondary details that can wait until later.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. Good AI UX is not just about aesthetics. The frontend should model explicit states, show progress or partial output when possible, and give the user clear recovery actions like stop, retry, or edit and resubmit.

seo.title: What are common AI UX challenges in frontend apps?
seo.description: Common AI UX challenges include latency, uncertainty, streaming states, trust, and graceful handling of errors or refusals.

title: What is prompt engineering, and how does it relate to frontend features?
description: This checks if you understand that prompts are part of product design and can be influenced by frontend UX and input shaping.

blocks.text: Definition (above the fold) Prompt engineering is the discipline of shaping instructions, constraints, and context so an AI model returns useful output consistently. In product teams, this directly relates to frontend features because the UI decides what structure users provide, what context is attached, and what safety rules are enforced before requests are sent. If the frontend collects ambiguous inputs, output quality drops even when the backend model is strong. Strong candidates also mention prompt versioning and output-quality observability, because model behavior drifts over time in production.

Core mental model Treat the prompt as a generated artifact, not free text. A reliable frontend composes prompt parts from validated fields: goal, format, constraints, context attachments, and output checks.

blocks.caption: Frontend controls are prompt-quality controls.

blocks.text: Runnable example #1: template assembly from UI state
This approach makes prompts auditable and easier to debug than raw free-text user input.
Runnable example #2: lightweight guardrail before send
Guardrails reduce obvious injection patterns and keep prompt size bounded. This is not complete security, but it materially lowers avoidable failures.

Common pitfalls
  • Allowing hidden prompt-injection text from pasted content or attachments without sanitization.
  • Sending unbounded context, which degrades model focus and increases latency/cost.
  • No prompt inspection/debug UI, so teams cannot explain why outputs changed between releases.

When to use / when not to use Use strict templates when you need consistency, compliance, or deterministic UX. Use flexible free-form prompting when exploration and creativity matter more than format guarantees. Do not expose fully unconstrained prompting in production workflows that require auditability or policy enforcement.

Interview follow-ups Q1: How do you measure prompt quality? A: Track task success rate, retry rate, and format-compliance metrics by prompt version. Q2: How do you prevent regressions? A: Keep prompt templates versioned and run golden test prompts in CI. Q3: Why is frontend involved? A: Frontend controls input structure, context attachment, and guardrails that directly shape model behavior.

Implementation checklist / takeaway Design UI controls as prompt controls, version templates, add guardrails, and evaluate outcomes with explicit metrics. That is the practical bridge between prompt engineering and frontend product quality.

incidentCard.title: Prompt Shaping Check
incidentCard.scenario: A team builds an AI feature where users paste anything into one large text box and send it directly to the model. Another teammate proposes breaking the UI into task, format, constraints, and context fields, trimming oversized context, and versioning the prompt template so output changes can be tracked over time. Which explanation is better?

options.label: A single freeform text area is usually better because structure gets in the user's way. If the model is strong enough, prompt quality should not depend much on frontend controls or template versioning.
Prompt engineering is mostly a backend concern. The frontend should just collect raw text and let the model figure out the task, format, and safety boundaries on its own.
Treat the prompt as a generated artifact. Structured UI fields, bounded context, guardrails, and prompt versioning make outputs more consistent, easier to debug, and safer in production.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The frontend directly shapes prompt quality by controlling how task, format, constraints, and context are collected. Strong setups also limit noisy context, add basic guardrails, and version prompt templates so regressions are observable.

seo.title: What is prompt engineering in frontend applications?
seo.description: Prompt engineering in frontend apps means structuring user input, context, and constraints to improve model response quality and safety.

title: Stack vs Queue in JavaScript (Differences + Implementation)
description: Choose a stack when the newest item should leave first and a queue when work must stay FIFO. Strong JavaScript answers surface the Array.shift() O(n) trap early and compare naive queues with head-index or two-stack implementations.

blocks.text: The Core Idea Choose by removal order first, then by implementation cost. A stack removes the newest item first (LIFO) and maps cleanly to push()/pop(). A queue removes the oldest item first (FIFO), but in JavaScript a naive queue often uses shift(), which can be O(n) because the array is re-indexed.

First implementation decision

If the most recent item should come out first, use stack semantics. If work must leave in submission order, use queue semantics. After that, decide whether a tiny demo can live with plain array methods or whether a real queue needs a head index or two-stack design to keep dequeue cheap.

Implementation trap
A queue written as items.push(v) plus items.shift() is easy to explain, but it is usually the wrong default once the queue grows or runs in a hot path.

When to use which

- Stack: call stack, undo/redo, backtracking, recursive traversal. - Queue: job processing, BFS, notification delivery, event buffering. - Same data, different removal policy: the bug is often in the order choice, not the container name.

Pitfalls and real-world choice

- Array.shift() is O(n) because it re-indexes the remaining items. - If you use a head index, compact occasionally so old slots do not accumulate forever. - Do not mix stack and queue semantics in one API just because both can use an array.

If your bug is about order, ask which item must leave first. Once the order is correct, then choose the cheapest implementation that preserves that rule.

Summary - Stack = LIFO and usually fits plain push()/pop(). - Queue = FIFO, but a naive push()/shift() queue can make dequeue O(n). - For real queues, prefer a head index or two-stack implementation when throughput matters. - Choose the structure by removal order first, then by implementation cost.

incidentCard.title: Processing Order Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate is building a job runner for image uploads. Jobs should be processed in the same order users submitted them, but the current code uses `push()` and `pop()`. Another teammate says this needs queue semantics instead, and that using `shift()` on a large array can become a performance problem unless you keep a head index or use two stacks. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Use queue semantics, but the simplest production approach is always `push()` and `shift()`. JavaScript engines optimize `shift()` enough that queue performance does not matter in practice.
Use a queue because uploads should run first-in, first-out. If performance matters, implement dequeue with a head index or two stacks instead of relying on `Array.shift()`.
Keep the stack because `push()` and `pop()` are O(1). The newest upload should naturally run first, and order is not a real queue concern if all jobs finish eventually.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key decision is access order: a queue is FIFO, so it matches job processing in submission order. For larger workloads, avoid paying `shift()` reindexing cost on every dequeue and use a head index or two-stack queue instead.

seo.title: Stack vs queue in JavaScript: what is the difference?
seo.description: Choose a stack when the latest item should leave first. Choose a queue when order must stay FIFO, and avoid Array.shift() for larger JavaScript queues.

title: Big-O Notation (Time and Space Complexity)
description: Big O is useful when you compare scaling trade-offs, not when you recite symbols. Strong JavaScript answers connect complexity to real performance decisions, hidden constants, and the common mistake of optimizing the wrong bottleneck.

blocks.text: Performance lens Big-O is not a badge for memorizing O(n log n). It is a way to reason about how an algorithm scales as input grows. The common mistake is treating it as the only performance signal and ignoring hidden constants, memory cost, or whether the real bottleneck is somewhere else.

Time vs space

Always say which complexity you mean. Example: mergesort is O(n log n) time but O(n) extra space.

Rules of thumb

- Sequential steps add: O(n) + O(n) -> O(n). - Nested loops multiply: O(n) * O(n) -> O(n^2). - Drop constants/lower-order terms: O(3n + 10) -> O(n). - Log base does not matter in Big-O.

Best/average/worst

A hash map is O(1) average but can degrade to O(n) worst-case. State which case you are discussing.

Amortized complexity

Dynamic array push is O(1) amortized because occasional O(n) resizes are rare.

Pitfalls

- Forgetting hidden loops (e.g., inside array methods). - Ignoring recursion depth or extra space. - Mixing average and worst-case without saying which.
Summary - Big-O describes growth rate as input grows. - Use rules to derive complexity quickly. - Be explicit about time vs space and best/average/worst-case.

incidentCard.title: Growth Rate Check
incidentCard.scenario: A dashboard feels fine with 50 rows but becomes painfully slow with 10,000. The render path loops through every row and, inside that loop, scans the full list again to find related items. One teammate says the slowdown is mostly about a bad constant factor. Another says the real issue is quadratic growth, so the code gets dramatically worse as input size increases. Which explanation is better?

options.label: The nested full-list scan makes the work grow roughly like O(n^2). Big-O matters here because the dominant growth rate explains why the slowdown becomes much worse as the dataset gets larger.
This is mainly about JavaScript being a bit slow in the browser. The algorithm is still basically linear, so the fix is mostly micro-optimizing syntax and hoping the engine inlines more code.
Big-O is too theoretical for UI work. As long as the feature works on small local data, complexity analysis can wait until much later because frontend performance is mostly a network problem.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. Big-O is about how work grows with input size, not just how fast the code feels on a small sample. Nested loops over the same collection often mean quadratic behavior, which is exactly why performance collapses as the dataset grows.

seo.title: Big O in JavaScript interviews: scaling trade-offs, hidden costs, and common mistakes
seo.description: Learn Big O through the performance-decision lens: how growth rate shapes scalability, where hidden constants still matter, and which common complexity mistakes confuse interviews.

title: Async Race Conditions and Stale UI Updates
description: Debug the production bug where async requests resolve out of order and overwrite newer UI state. The fix is cancellation, request identity, shared-controller ownership, or takeLatest-style guards that stop stale results from winning.

blocks.text: The core issue This is a classic production debug problem in search, filters, autosave flows, and route-driven detail pages. Request A starts, then request B starts, B finishes first, and the UI briefly looks correct. When A finishes later, it overwrites newer state with stale data unless your code cancels or guards the older request.

rows: UI is overwritten with stale 'rea' results
blocks.caption: Classic search-as-you-type race condition; autosave and navigation races behave the same way.

blocks.text: How to prevent it You need either real cancellation or a stale-result guard. Both prevent old responses from winning the race.

rows: Cancel the previous request so it never resolves
Best when the API supports AbortSignal (e.g., fetch)
Only apply results if the id matches the latest
Works even if the API cannot be cancelled
Wrap calls to auto-cancel or ignore stale results
Common in RxJS or custom utilities
blocks.caption: Three reliable ways to avoid stale updates.

blocks.text: When the async work cannot be aborted AbortController only helps when the underlying task listens to AbortSignal. Autosave pipelines, IndexedDB work, and some library promises still need a latest-version guard so stale completions cannot win.

Shared-controller follow-up If one route transition starts several fetches, a single AbortController can own the whole screen load. Aborting on route leave cancels profile, permissions, and related requests together instead of leaking old work into the next screen.
Pitfalls - Promise.race does not cancel the losing promises. - Debounce reduces request count but does not prevent out-of-order responses. - Always clean up abort listeners or timers to avoid leaks.

incidentCard.title: Stale Results Check
incidentCard.scenario: A search box fires a request for `rea`, then the user quickly types `react`. The `react` response arrives first, but a moment later the older `rea` response overwrites the UI. One teammate wants to just add a debounce and call it fixed. Another says the code needs cancellation or a latest-request guard so older responses cannot win. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Use an AbortController or a request-id guard so only the newest request can update the UI. Debounce may reduce traffic, but it does not stop older in-flight responses from arriving later and overwriting fresh state.
Add a debounce and leave the rest alone. Once requests happen less often, out-of-order responses stop being a real problem, so explicit stale-response handling is unnecessary.
Wrap the requests in `Promise.race()` so the first one to finish becomes the correct answer. That automatically cancels the slower promise and guarantees the UI always shows the newest result.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The bug is caused by async requests resolving out of order. Debounce can lower request volume, but you still need cancellation or a stale-result guard so an older response cannot overwrite newer UI state.

seo.title: Debug async race conditions: stale UI, cancellation, and latest-result guards
seo.description: Prevent stale UI updates in production with AbortController, request identity guards, shared-controller ownership, and takeLatest-style patterns.

title: Web Workers: Off-Main-Thread JavaScript
description: Explain what Web Workers are, how message passing works (structured clone + Transferables), and the key constraints (no DOM). Include a real scenario, trade-offs (startup + message overhead), and common pitfalls like leaks or copying huge payloads.

blocks.text: What is a Web Worker? A Web Worker runs JavaScript on a separate thread from the main UI thread. Use it for CPU-heavy work (parsing, compression, image processing) so the UI stays responsive and scrolling stays smooth.

Real-world scenario Example: you need to parse a 50MB JSON export and compute stats while the user keeps scrolling. Doing this on the main thread will jank the UI. Offload the parsing + aggregation to a worker, then send the small result back to the UI thread.

blocks.caption: Workers are for computation, not UI rendering.

blocks.text: How communication works Workers communicate via postMessage. Data is copied using the structured clone algorithm, or transferred using Transferables (e.g., ArrayBuffer) to avoid expensive copies. Functions and DOM nodes cannot be cloned.

Lifecycle + error handling Workers are long-lived by default. Terminate them when done (worker.terminate()) to avoid leaks. Handle errors with worker.onerror and worker.onmessageerror so failures do not silently disappear.

When to use (and when not to) Use workers for CPU-heavy tasks or large data transforms. Avoid them for small tasks where the startup cost + message overhead outweighs the benefit. For I/O-bound work (fetching), a worker usually does not help unless you also do heavy processing on the results.

Common pitfalls interviewers look for - Forgetting that workers cannot touch the DOM. - Copying large payloads instead of using Transferables. - Spawning too many workers (startup + memory cost). - Not terminating workers or handling errors. - Assuming every API is available inside a worker (environment-dependent).

incidentCard.title: Main Thread Relief Check
incidentCard.scenario: A page freezes while parsing a huge JSON export and calculating statistics. One teammate wants to move the parsing work into a Web Worker, send the file data with `postMessage`, and return only the computed result to the UI thread. Another teammate says the worker should update the DOM directly so the page can stay interactive. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Use a Web Worker for the CPU-heavy parsing and aggregation, then pass results back to the main thread. Workers help keep the UI responsive, but they communicate by messages and cannot manipulate the DOM directly.

Move both the parsing and the DOM updates into the worker. Since the worker runs off the main thread, it is the best place to directly render progress and update the page while the main thread stays idle.
Do not use a worker here because JavaScript is already asynchronous. As long as the file arrives through async APIs, the parsing step will not block the UI enough to justify off-thread work.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. Web Workers are useful when CPU-heavy work would otherwise block the main thread. They can process data and send messages back, but they do not have DOM access, so rendering still belongs on the main UI thread.

seo.title: What are Web Workers in JavaScript?
seo.description: Web Workers run JavaScript in background threads so CPU-heavy work does not block the main UI thread.

title: Escaping vs Sanitizing: What's the Difference?
description: Explain the difference between output escaping (context-specific encoding) and sanitizing user input (allowlist filtering). Include when to use each, how they work, and why both matter for XSS prevention.

blocks.text: Big idea Escaping and sanitizing both reduce XSS risk, but they solve different problems. Escaping encodes characters so the browser treats user input as text in a specific output context. Sanitizing removes or rewrites dangerous parts of user-provided markup so some HTML can be allowed.

rows: Encode special characters so they render as text
Allow some HTML while blocking dangerous tags/attrs
Context-specific (HTML text, attributes, URLs, JS, CSS)
DOMPurify, browser sanitizer APIs, server allowlists
Wrong encoder for the context can still allow XSS
Weak allowlist can allow XSS or strip too much content
blocks.caption: Escaping encodes for a context; sanitizing filters to an allowlist.

blocks.text: Real-world scenario A profile bio can be plain text (escape and render safely), but a blog editor might allow limited rich text (sanitize with an allowlist). Decide based on the product requirement, not convenience.

rows: Avoid inline JS; never concatenate user input
blocks.caption: Encoding is context-specific. Use the right output channel.

blocks.text: Common pitfalls - Stripping tags with regex (easy to bypass). - Sanitizing once and reusing the result in a different context. - Double-escaping by encoding the same value twice. - Storing only sanitized HTML and losing the original data.
Summary Escape for the specific output context. Sanitize only when you must allow markup, and pair it with defense-in-depth like CSP and Trusted Types.

incidentCard.title: Safe Rendering Check
incidentCard.scenario: A team is adding user bios. For the plain profile header, the product only needs text. For the long-form editor, the product wants to allow a small subset of rich text like links and bold tags. One teammate says to sanitize everything and always inject with `innerHTML`. Another says plain text should be escaped through safe text APIs, and sanitizing should be reserved for places where limited markup is intentionally allowed. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Sanitize all user content once and always render it with `innerHTML`. That way you only need one security rule, and escaping becomes unnecessary because the sanitizer already handled everything.
The simplest fix is to remove angle brackets with a regex before rendering. If most tags are gone, escaping and sanitizing become mostly interchangeable from a security standpoint.

Use escaping or safe text APIs like `textContent` for plain text, because the browser should treat the value as text. Only sanitize when the feature intentionally allows some HTML, and then apply an allowlist-based sanitizer before rendering.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. Escaping and sanitizing solve different problems. Escape when the value should render as text in a specific context, and sanitize only when the product deliberately allows some markup and you need to filter it through a safe allowlist.

seo.title: Escaping vs sanitizing: what is the difference?
seo.description: Escaping encodes data for a specific output context, while sanitizing removes or allows only safe content.

title: DOM XSS Prevention in JavaScript: Dangerous Sinks, Safe APIs, and Trusted Types
description: Security-focused frontend interview guide to DOM XSS: attacker-controlled sources like location.hash or postMessage, dangerous DOM sinks, safe rendering patterns, URL validation, and defense in depth with CSP and Trusted Types.

blocks.text: Definition (above the fold) DOM XSS occurs when untrusted input reaches a DOM sink that interprets it as code or HTML. This can happen entirely on the client side without a server-side template bug, for example when location.hash, postMessage, or a profile-bio field gets pushed into innerHTML after page load. The fix is data-flow control: identify untrusted sources, block dangerous sinks, and enforce safe APIs by default.

Core mental model Model this as source -> transform -> sink. If source is attacker-controlled and sink interprets executable content, you have risk. Break the chain by sanitizing/validating at boundaries and preferring non-executable sink APIs.

DOM XSS vs reflected/stored XSS Reflected or stored XSS often starts when the server sends unsafe HTML back to the browser. DOM XSS starts later: client-side code reads data such as location.hash, postMessage, or API content and feeds it into a dangerous sink. Same attacker outcome, different bug location and debugging path.

blocks.caption: Treat all external/user-derived strings as untrusted by default.

blocks.text: Runnable example #1: dangerous sink vs safe text rendering
Using textContent prevents markup execution and should be the default for user-controlled text.
Runnable example #2: URL sink validation allowlist

rows: Only sanitize when rich HTML is required
Template rendering with trusted static markup only
Avoid dynamic script construction from input
blocks.caption: Prefer APIs that do not evaluate or parse attacker-controlled code paths.

blocks.text:
Common pitfalls
  • Assuming framework escaping protects every manual DOM operation.
  • Sanitizing once but later mutating string into another unsafe sink.
  • Missing origin checks on postMessage handlers.
  • Relying on blacklist regex rules instead of structured parsing/allowlists.

Defense in depth Use CSP to restrict script execution and reduce exploit impact if a sink slips through. Adopt Trusted Types in large apps to force controlled creation of HTML/script URLs. Pair client controls with server-side output encoding and validation for full coverage.

Escaping vs sanitization vs Trusted Types Escaping is context-specific output encoding. Sanitization removes unsafe markup when you intentionally allow limited HTML. Trusted Types does something different: it makes risky sinks accept only approved objects, so raw strings cannot quietly reach innerHTML or similar APIs.
Runnable example #3: sanitize once, enforce at the sink

Interview follow-ups Q1: Is escaping enough for all sinks? A: No, sink context matters; URL, HTML, and script contexts differ. Q2: Why is DOM XSS tricky? A: It can be introduced entirely in client code after data leaves the server. Q3: First practical hardening step? A: Replace dangerous sinks with safe defaults and add CSP policy reporting.

Practical scenario
A support widget reads location.hash to restore UI state and also renders agent notes from an API. If either value reaches innerHTML without sanitization or a trusted-types policy, an attacker-controlled link or stored profile note can execute in the agent dashboard.

What to do
Keep plain text on textContent, validate structured URLs separately, and gate rich HTML behind a sanitizer plus Trusted Types/CSP.

Implementation checklist / takeaway Map sources and sinks, block dangerous DOM APIs by default, validate structured inputs (especially URLs), and enforce CSP/Trusted Types where possible. Strong interview answers focus on secure data flow, not just one sanitizer call.

incidentCard.title: DOM Sink Check
incidentCard.scenario: A page reads `q` from the URL and shows it in a results header. One teammate suggests assigning the value to `innerHTML` because it is quick and 'the server already escaped things somewhere.' Another teammate says the client should still treat URL data as untrusted, render plain text with a safe API like `textContent`, and validate structured values such as links before putting them into DOM sinks. Which explanation is better?

options.label: The main rule is to run every string through one sanitizer helper before any DOM write. After that, sink choice does not matter much because the helper already neutralized the dangerous content.
Treat client-side sources like URL params as untrusted. Use safe sinks such as `textContent` for text, and validate structured values like URLs before assigning them to attributes like `href`.

Using `innerHTML` is acceptable here because the value originally came through server-side escaping. Once a string has been cleaned earlier in the stack, the client can reuse it in any DOM sink without extra checks.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. DOM XSS is about untrusted data reaching a risky sink on the client. Safe defaults like `textContent` break that chain for text, while URLs and other structured values need their own validation rules rather than one generic sanitizer.

seo.title: How do you prevent DOM XSS: sources, sinks, sanitization, and Trusted Types
seo.description: Prevent DOM XSS by tracing attacker-controlled sources to dangerous sinks, using safe DOM APIs, validating URLs, and layering sanitization with Trusted Types.

title: Testing Strategy: Edge Cases and Boundaries
description: Testing edge cases is about finding boundary, invariant, and failure-mode bugs with a small high-signal test set. The real production pitfall is writing many happy-path tests while missing the inputs that actually break logic.

blocks.text: High-signal goal A good edge-case strategy is not about more tests. It is about choosing the smallest set that still finds boundary bugs, invalid-input bugs, and failure modes before production does. The common mistake is maximizing test count instead of bug-finding power.

rows: Group similar inputs to reduce tests
add a test for every fix
blocks.caption: Cover the space with intent, not volume.

blocks.text: Practical checklist 1) List inputs and outputs. 2) Identify constraints (min/max, allowed chars, allowed states). 3) Pick boundaries and one representative per equivalence class. 4) Add invalid and unexpected types. 5) Add a real-world case and a worst-case case. 6) Add regression tests for any previous bugs.

rows: assert on UTC or iso strings
blocks.caption: JavaScript-specific edge cases to call out explicitly.

blocks.text: Common anti-patterns - Only testing happy paths. - Too many tests that assert the same behavior. - Tests that mirror the implementation instead of the requirements. - Random tests without a seed (non-deterministic failures).
Summary Design tests around boundaries, invariants, and failure modes. Use a small, deliberate set of cases that make bugs obvious and keep the suite fast and reliable.

incidentCard.title: Edge-Case Coverage Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate writes 40 tests for a `clamp(value, min, max)` helper, but almost all of them check ordinary middle values. Another teammate suggests a much smaller set: `min-1`, `min`, one valid middle value, `max`, `max+1`, plus a few invalid cases like `null` or `NaN`, and one invariant test that output always stays within range. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Use a small, intentional set that covers boundaries, representative valid inputs, invalid inputs, and invariants. That usually catches more real bugs than a long list of repetitive happy-path tests.
Keep the 40 tests because quantity is the main goal. Even if many cases are similar, more examples always give stronger confidence than a smaller boundary-focused set.
Focus on common production inputs and skip awkward edge cases like `min-1` or `NaN`. If users mostly send normal values, boundary and invalid-input tests add noise more than value.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. Strong test strategy is about coverage with intent, not raw volume. Boundaries, equivalence classes, invalid inputs, invariants, and regression cases are usually where bugs hide, so a compact set can outperform dozens of repetitive happy-path assertions.

seo.title: Testing edge cases in JavaScript: boundaries, failure modes, and high-signal test strategy
seo.description: Explain edge-case testing through the real bug-prevention lens: boundaries, invalid inputs, invariants, and the common mistake of shipping happy-path-only coverage.

title: Performance Profiling Workflow (Browser DevTools)
description: Use a production profiling workflow: pick the user-facing metric, capture a trace, isolate the slowest CPU, render, or network stage, then debug that bottleneck before changing code.

blocks.text: Goal Profiling turns a vague "the page feels slow" into one bottleneck you can debug. The production workflow is repeatable: pick the user-facing metric, capture the same scenario, identify the slowest stage, fix that first, and verify the result with the exact same interaction instead of guessing.

rows: UI responds to user input fast
blocks.caption: Start with user-facing metrics before micro-optimizations.

rows: Create a stable scenario (same route, same data)
Record interactions and mark with User Timing
Find long tasks, layout thrash, heavy scripting
Look at the flame chart + Main thread
Re-run the same trace and compare
blocks.caption: A repeatable workflow beats random tweaks.

rows: Too much JS on main thread
blocks.caption: Use the trace to map signals to fixes.

blocks.text: Common pitfalls - Optimizing without a baseline metric. - Profiling in dev builds only (prod bundles behave differently). - Ignoring long tasks and focusing on tiny wins. - Not re-testing after changes.
Summary Pick a metric, record a trace, fix the biggest bottleneck, and verify with the same scenario. That loop is the fastest path to real-world performance gains.

incidentCard.title: Measure First Check
incidentCard.scenario: A product page feels laggy when users open filters and scroll. One teammate wants to immediately add memoization, debounce handlers, and lazy-load more code 'just in case.' Another teammate says to first reproduce the issue in a stable scenario, record a DevTools Performance trace, look for long tasks or layout thrash, apply the biggest fix, and then re-run the same test while checking metrics like INP or LCP. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Start with a repeatable profiling workflow: reproduce, record a trace, isolate the main bottleneck, fix it, and verify with the same scenario and user-facing metrics. That avoids random tweaks and tells you whether the change actually helped.
Skip tracing and apply common optimizations immediately. Performance work moves faster when you assume the usual culprits and make several fixes at once instead of spending time measuring.
Use the page manually after each change and trust how it feels. Browser metrics and traces are useful mostly for backend-heavy problems, but frontend performance is easier to judge by eye.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. Strong profiling work starts with a stable reproduction and a user-facing metric, then uses traces to identify the biggest bottleneck before changing code. The final step matters too: re-measure the same scenario so you know the fix improved real performance instead of just changing the code.

seo.title: Profile JavaScript performance: DevTools workflow, bottlenecks, and debug steps
seo.description: Profile JavaScript performance with a production workflow: record the same scenario, find the slowest bottleneck, debug it with traces, and verify the exact fix.

title: JavaScript Memory Leak Debugging: Common Sources, DevTools Workflow, and Fixes
description: Interview-ready guide to debugging JavaScript memory leaks in browser apps: event listeners, timers, detached DOM nodes, unbounded caches, and a repeatable Chrome DevTools workflow using heap snapshots and retaining paths.

blocks.text: Definition (above the fold) A JavaScript memory leak happens when objects remain reachable after they should be discarded. The garbage collector only frees unreachable objects, so leaks are usually retention bugs, not GC bugs. In long-lived frontend apps, leaks show up as rising memory, slower interactions, and eventual tab crashes.

Core mental model Think in terms of graph reachability: roots -> references -> objects. If any root path still points to an object (global store, listener, timer, closure, cache), that object survives collection. Fixes remove those retention paths.

rows: Clear timers and null related refs
JS references keep removed nodes alive
Drop references when nodes are removed
Use size limits, TTL, or LRU policies
blocks.caption: Most leaks are lifecycle cleanup failures.

blocks.text: Runnable example #1: listener + timer lifecycle cleanup
A deterministic dispose() path is one of the most reliable anti-leak patterns for UI components and utilities.
Runnable example #2: bounded cache to prevent unbounded growth

rows: Heap size and dominant object types
Objects persist when feature should be disposed
Path points to listener, timer, or global cache
blocks.caption: Use memory profiling as evidence, not guesswork.

blocks.text:
Common pitfalls
  • Relying on framework unmount without verifying custom listeners/timers are cleaned.
  • Keeping debug data in global arrays/maps in production builds.
  • Capturing large objects in long-lived closures unnecessarily.
  • Treating one snapshot as proof instead of comparing before/after interaction loops.

When to use / when not to use Use deep memory profiling when users report progressive slowdown, battery drain, or tab crashes after repeated flows. For one-time short pages, quick cleanup audits may be enough. Avoid premature micro-optimization if memory behavior is stable and bounded under realistic sessions.

Interview follow-ups Q1: How do you prove a leak, not just temporary growth? A: Repeat the action many times and compare snapshots after idle GC windows. Q2: What leaks most in SPAs? A: Forgotten listener/subscription cleanup and unbounded caches. Q3: First production safeguard? A: Add disposal contracts and bounded cache policies by default.

Implementation checklist / takeaway Make cleanup explicit, bound all caches, profile with baseline/compare/retainers, and verify fixes with repeated interaction scenarios. Strong interview answers combine memory model clarity with a practical debugging workflow.

incidentCard.title: Leak Source Check
incidentCard.scenario: A modal opens and closes many times during a session. After a while the tab gets slower and memory keeps climbing. One teammate blames the garbage collector for 'not freeing things fast enough.' Another says the likely issue is that listeners, timers, or other references still point at modal state after close, so the objects remain reachable and never become collectible. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Take one heap snapshot after memory grows and look at the largest object. If the snapshot looks big once, that is enough to prove a leak without repeating the interaction or checking retaining paths.

Treat it as a retention bug first. Event listeners, intervals, subscriptions, detached DOM references, or unbounded caches can keep modal state reachable, so the fix is explicit cleanup plus snapshot comparison and retainers analysis.
This is mainly a garbage-collector timing issue. If the GC does not reclaim memory quickly enough, the safest fix is usually to force more collections or wait longer between modal opens.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. Memory leaks in frontend apps are usually caused by objects staying reachable through listeners, timers, closures, subscriptions, or caches. The practical workflow is to reproduce the interaction repeatedly, compare heap snapshots, inspect retaining paths, and then remove the references keeping the objects alive.

seo.title: What causes memory leaks in JavaScript apps?
seo.description: Common JavaScript memory leaks come from lingering references such as event listeners, timers, detached DOM nodes, and unbounded caches.

title: ESM vs CommonJS in JavaScript: Interop, Packaging, and Build Behavior
description: Complete interview answer for ESM vs CommonJS covering static vs dynamic loading, live bindings, Node package exports, interop pitfalls, and why module format affects bundling, optimization, and runtime behavior.

blocks.text: Definition (above the fold) ES Modules (ESM) are the standard JavaScript module system with static import/export syntax that tools can analyze ahead of time. CommonJS (CJS) is the older Node.js module system using require and module.exports. Interviewers care less about syntax and more about interop and packaging decisions in real projects.

Mental model Think of ESM as statically analyzable graph edges and CJS as runtime-evaluated module loading. Static analysis enables better tree-shaking and optimization. Runtime flexibility in CJS can help legacy systems but complicates analysis.

rows: Static graph known at build time
Best for modern bundlers and browsers
blocks.caption: Choose format based on ecosystem, runtime, and optimization goals.

blocks.text: Runnable example #1: equivalent module exports
Same behavior is possible in both systems, but the package boundary and import style define interoperability and optimization quality.
Runnable example #2: package exports map for safer consumption

rows: undefined is not a function at runtime
Align export style and import syntax explicitly
Consumers resolve wrong file in bundlers
Works in dev but breaks in production runtime
blocks.caption: Most interview follow-ups focus on these production failures.

blocks.text:
Common pitfalls
  • Publishing ambiguous package entrypoints without an exports map.
  • Assuming all tools resolve CJS/ESM the same way.
  • Using CJS patterns in ESM codebases and losing optimization opportunities.
  • Not testing package consumption in both import and require scenarios.

When to use / when not to use Use ESM by default for modern frontend and new libraries. Keep CJS compatibility only when you must support legacy runtimes or tooling. Do not keep dual formats indefinitely without ownership, because dual-package maintenance can create drift and subtle bugs.

Interview follow-ups Q1: Why does ESM help optimization? A: Static imports allow bundlers to prune unused code more safely. Q2: How do you ship both formats? A: Build dual outputs and define deterministic exports conditions. Q3: What breaks most often? A: Default/named interop mismatches and inconsistent runtime/tool resolver behavior.

Implementation checklist / takeaway Prefer ESM-first packaging, define explicit exports conditions, test import/require consumers, and avoid ambiguous entrypoints. Strong interview answers connect module format to runtime correctness and bundle quality.

incidentCard.title: Module Interop Check
incidentCard.scenario: A shared package works in one app but fails in another with a default-vs-named import error. One teammate says ESM and CommonJS are basically interchangeable, so the safest fix is to keep mixing `require`, `module.exports`, and `import` until it works everywhere. Another teammate says the package should define explicit `exports` conditions, align import syntax with actual export shape, and test both `import` and `require` consumers. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Treat module format as a real packaging boundary. Use explicit `exports` conditions, keep export style and import syntax aligned, and test both ESM and CommonJS consumers so interop bugs do not hide until runtime.

Assume ESM and CommonJS are close enough that bundlers will smooth over the differences. As long as the package exposes some kind of JS file, consumers can usually mix default imports, named imports, and `require` without much risk.

Lean on dynamic `require()` in shared packages because it is more flexible than static imports. That flexibility matters more than analyzability, and bundlers can still tree-shake most of the result if needed.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. ESM and CommonJS differ in loading model, analyzability, and interop behavior. The practical fix is to make package entrypoints explicit, keep export/import shapes consistent, and validate both `import` and `require` consumption instead of hoping tools resolve the mismatch the same way.

seo.title: ESM vs CommonJS in JavaScript: what is the difference?
seo.description: ESM uses static import or export syntax for modern module loading, while CommonJS uses require and module.exports at runtime.

title: Tree-Shaking: How It Works and Common Pitfalls
description: Tree shaking is dead-code elimination, but real bundler results depend on ESM, side-effect declarations, and import shape. The production pitfall is assuming unused code disappears automatically when package structure still blocks elimination.

blocks.text: Bundler pitfall Tree-shaking is not a magic "remove everything unused" switch. Bundlers need static ESM structure and trustworthy side-effect signals. When bundles stay fat, the debug question is usually where hidden side effects, barrel exports, or import shape prevented elimination.

rows: Unused code can be safely removed
blocks.caption: Tree-shaking needs static structure + side-effect info.
Most tree-shaking failures are about side effects or dynamic loading.

blocks.text: How to verify Use a bundle analyzer to compare sizes before/after. In devtools, inspect the output bundle to ensure unused exports are removed. Add perf budgets to prevent regressions.
Summary Tree-shaking is effective when your code is ESM, side-effect free, and statically analyzable. If bundles stay large, check for CJS dependencies and unmarked side effects.

incidentCard.title: Bundle Pruning Check
incidentCard.scenario: A team imports one helper from a shared package, but the production bundle still pulls in almost the whole library. One teammate says tree-shaking should remove unused code automatically no matter how the package is built. Another teammate says tree-shaking works best when the package uses static ESM exports, avoids hidden side effects, and marks side effects accurately so the bundler can safely drop unused modules. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Modern bundlers are smart enough to remove unused code in almost any module format. If the app imports only one helper, tree-shaking should work even if the package relies on CommonJS or side-effectful barrel files.
Tree-shaking is a minor detail compared with minification. As long as the build is minified, module format and side-effect metadata do not meaningfully change how much dead code stays in the output.

Tree-shaking depends on static ESM structure and trustworthy side-effect information. If code is CommonJS, dynamically imported, or has side effects the bundler cannot safely ignore, unused pieces often stay in the bundle.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. Tree-shaking is not guaranteed just because a bundler exists. It relies on statically analyzable ESM graphs and accurate side-effect signals so unused exports can be removed safely. CommonJS, dynamic loading, and hidden side effects often block that optimization.

seo.title: Tree shaking in bundlers: ESM requirements, side-effect pitfalls, and bundle debug
seo.description: Explain tree shaking through the real bundler pitfall: ESM helps, side effects block elimination, and verification matters when bundles stay large after optimizing.

title: JavaScript Intl.DateTimeFormat: Time Zones, DST, and Reliable Date Formatting
description: Intl.DateTimeFormat solves the production bug of formatting across time zones and DST without unsafe manual offset math. The useful angle is IANA zone IDs, locale formatting, and why fixed offsets break scheduling or audit displays.

blocks.text: Production bug to avoid Time-zone formatting gets hard when daylight-saving transitions, locale rules, and business zones differ from the user's machine. Intl.DateTimeFormat exists so you do not ship fixed-offset math that works in March and fails in November.

Core mental model Store timestamps in UTC, transport as ISO strings, and convert at the presentation edge with an IANA time zone (for example America/New_York). Do not store local wall-clock strings as source-of-truth.

rows: Correct locale grammar and DST handling
Pass explicit IANA zone in formatter options
Stable token extraction without brittle regex
Compare UTC timestamps, not formatted strings
blocks.caption: Keep UTC for logic, Intl formatting for display.

blocks.text: Runnable example #1: stable zone-aware formatting
This safely formats around DST boundaries without hardcoding offsets like UTC-8 or UTC-7.
Runnable example #2: formatToParts for composable UI

rows: Parse ISO input, format only for output
Using display value for data logic
Persist UTC and explicit zone metadata
blocks.caption: Most production date bugs are data-modeling mistakes, not syntax mistakes.

blocks.text:
Common pitfalls
  • Storing date-only values as midnight local time and getting off-by-one day bugs across regions.
  • Rendering with browser default zone when product requirements need business-zone consistency.
  • Testing only one locale/time zone and missing DST boundary regressions.

When to use / when not to use Use Intl for display formatting in browser and server-rendered UI. Use timestamp math for calculations and persistence. Do not rely on manually concatenated date strings for mission-critical workflows like billing cutoffs or booking windows.

Interview follow-ups Q1: Why avoid fixed offsets? A: Offsets change with DST and differ across historical periods. Q2: How do you test this reliably? A: Add test cases around DST start/end for multiple IANA zones. Q3: What do you store in database? A: UTC instant plus optional business-zone metadata when needed.

Implementation checklist / takeaway Persist UTC, format with Intl + explicit IANA zones, validate DST edge cases in tests, and keep display concerns separate from business logic. Strong answers show both correctness and maintainability.

incidentCard.title: Timezone Correctness Check
incidentCard.scenario: A booking screen shows the wrong local time for some users after a daylight-saving change. One teammate wants to keep subtracting a fixed hour offset because 'the business is always UTC-5 anyway.' Another teammate says timestamps should stay in UTC, and the UI should format them with `Intl.DateTimeFormat` plus an explicit IANA zone like `America/New_York` or the user's real zone. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Use a fixed offset like UTC-5 or UTC-8 because business time usually does not move enough to justify a full time-zone solution. Manual offset math is simpler and should be stable as long as the region does not change often.

Save the already formatted local time string in storage and reuse it later. If the exact displayed string is preserved, you avoid timezone conversion bugs because the frontend no longer needs to calculate anything.

Keep the source-of-truth timestamp in UTC and format for display with `Intl.DateTimeFormat` using an explicit IANA time zone. That handles locale rules and daylight-saving transitions without fragile manual offset math.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. Fixed offsets break around DST changes and historical timezone rules. The robust pattern is to persist UTC for logic and transport, then format at the presentation edge with `Intl.DateTimeFormat` and an explicit IANA zone so display stays correct across regions and seasons.

seo.title: JavaScript time-zone formatting: DST bugs, IANA zones, and Intl.DateTimeFormat
seo.description: Explain Intl.DateTimeFormat through the production pitfall: DST shifts, fixed-offset bugs, IANA time zones, and reliable cross-region date rendering.

title: Mocks vs Stubs vs Spies (Test Doubles) in JavaScript
description: Explain the difference between mocks, stubs, and spies in unit testing. Describe when to use each, how to avoid brittle tests, and how dependency injection helps testability. Include an async example and common pitfalls.

blocks.text: Why this matters In interviews and real code, testing isn't about writing 200 assertions—it’s about verifying behavior at the right boundary. Test doubles (mocks/stubs/spies) let you isolate a unit from slow, flaky, or external dependencies (network, storage, time, analytics).

Practical scenario You have searchUsers(query) that calls an HTTP client and returns parsed JSON. In a unit test, you don’t want real network. You want to: - Stub the HTTP client to return a controlled response. - Spy on the client to assert it was called correctly. - Optionally mock the client to both define expectations and fail if they’re not met.

rows: You want to verify interactions at a boundary
Returns fake data to drive a code path
You need deterministic input (no real network/time)
A stub + expectations ("must be called with X")
You want strict interaction contracts for critical boundaries
