blocks.caption: Rule of thumb: prefer stubs for inputs, spies for boundary verification, mocks only when strict contracts are worth the brittleness.

blocks.text: Common pitfalls - Over-mocking: tests fail on harmless refactors because they assert internal steps, not outcomes. - Leaking globals: stubbing global.fetch or Date.now without restoring breaks other tests. - Async flakiness: tests that depend on real timers or network ordering become flaky. - Missing error paths: only testing the happy path hides retries/timeouts/empty responses bugs.

Trade-off to mention in interviews Unit tests are fast and isolate logic but can drift from reality if you mock too much. Integration tests catch wiring issues but are slower and harder to debug. A strong strategy uses both: - Unit tests for core logic + edge cases. - A few integration tests for the most important flows (auth, payments, critical UI paths).

Answer to land "I prefer dependency injection so I can stub inputs and spy on boundaries. I assert outputs first, then interactions only when they’re part of the contract (e.g., a retry policy or analytics call). Mocks are useful for strict contracts, but I avoid over-mocking because it makes tests brittle."

incidentCard.title: Test Double Choice Check
incidentCard.scenario: You are unit-testing a function that calls an HTTP helper and returns parsed users. One teammate wants to mock every internal call and assert the exact implementation steps. Another says to inject the HTTP helper, stub its response so the test stays deterministic, and optionally spy on the call boundary only if the request URL or retry behavior is part of the contract. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Avoid spies and stubs because they hide real behavior. Unit tests should call the actual network helper whenever possible so the test proves the whole flow works from end to end.

Prefer dependency injection, stub the external dependency to control inputs, and spy only on meaningful boundary interactions. Focus assertions on outputs first so tests stay useful without becoming brittle.
Mock everything inside the function and assert the full sequence of internal calls. The more implementation detail a unit test locks down, the more confidence you get when refactoring.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The goal of test doubles is to isolate the unit at the right boundary. Stubs provide controlled inputs, spies verify interactions when those interactions are part of the contract, and heavy mocking of internal steps often makes tests fragile for little benefit.

seo.title: Mocks vs stubs vs spies: what is the difference?
seo.description: Mocks verify interactions, stubs provide controlled responses, and spies observe calls while keeping original behavior or wrapping it.

title: Garbage Collection (GC) in JavaScript: Memory Pressure and Jank
description: Explain JavaScript garbage collection with a production angle: memory pressure, allocation churn, GC pauses, and how to debug jank with Chrome DevTools Performance and Memory panels.

blocks.text: Big idea JavaScript is garbage-collected, but production performance still depends on what you keep reachable and how much you allocate. If your app creates lots of short-lived objects or accidentally retains references, the GC has more work to do, and that shows up as jank. The useful interview angle is not just "the engine frees memory for me"; it is how you would debug allocation churn, memory pressure, and pause spikes.

rows: If something is reachable from roots (global, closures, DOM refs), GC won't collect it
Leaks are usually "still referenced", not "GC failed"
Heap grows → GC runs more often / more aggressively
Frequent GCs can compete with rendering
Some GC work stops the world briefly
Pauses can push frames over 16ms and hurt INP
blocks.caption: GC is automatic, but your allocation patterns and references decide the workload.

blocks.text: Practical scenario You render a feed and create new objects on every scroll (formatting strings, building derived arrays, cloning objects). The UI looks fine on a dev machine, but on mid-range devices you see stutters. Often the hidden cause is allocation churn: the GC runs repeatedly, causing small pauses that add up.

How to investigate in Chrome DevTools 1) Performance panel: record a trace while reproducing jank. - Look for long tasks, layout thrash, and also GC activity markers. 2) Memory panel: - Take heap snapshots before/after repeating an interaction. - Use Retainers to see what keeps objects alive. - Use Allocation instrumentation to find hot allocation sites. If memory keeps growing and never comes down after a "steady-state" interaction, you likely have a retention leak.

rows: JS references keep removed nodes alive
Don't store DOM nodes long-term; null references
blocks.caption: Most "memory leaks" are just references that never get released.

blocks.text: WeakMap / WeakRef: when they help - WeakMap keys are weakly held: if the key object becomes unreachable, the entry can be collected. - This is useful for memoization keyed by objects (DOM nodes, component instances) where you don't want your cache to keep them alive. They are not a silver bullet: you still need to remove event listeners, bound caches, and avoid retaining large graphs in closures.

Pitfalls - Treating GC as a fix: "it will clean up" → not if you still reference it. - Allocating aggressively in hot paths (scroll, animation frames). - Using caches without eviction or with high-cardinality keys. - Profiling only in dev builds (prod code can behave differently).

Answer to land "If the UI is janky after repeated interactions, I profile both CPU and memory. I look for allocation churn and GC markers in the Performance panel, then use heap snapshots and retainers to confirm what stays reachable. Fixes are usually: cleanup subscriptions/listeners, bound caches (LRU/TTL), and reduce allocations in hot paths."

incidentCard.title: GC Jank Check
incidentCard.scenario: A feed feels smooth at first, but after a few minutes of scrolling it starts stuttering. One teammate says garbage collection is automatic, so the slowdown must be somewhere else. Another says heavy allocation churn or retained objects can increase memory pressure, trigger more GC work, and show up as frame drops, so the right move is to profile both performance and memory before changing code. Which explanation is better?

options.label: Ignore garbage collection because it is fully automatic. If the browser owns memory management, GC cannot meaningfully contribute to UI jank, so performance work should focus only on rendering and networking.

Open the Memory panel once, take a single snapshot, and look for a big number. If heap usage seems high in that moment, you already know the cause, so repeated traces or before-and-after comparisons are unnecessary.

Treat GC-related jank as a signal of allocation patterns or retained references. Record a Performance trace for GC markers, inspect Memory snapshots and retainers, then reduce hot-path allocations and clean up references that should no longer stay reachable.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. GC is automatic, but your code still controls how much memory gets allocated and what stays reachable. Repeated allocations and retention bugs increase GC pressure, so the practical workflow is to inspect both Performance traces and Memory snapshots, then reduce churn and remove stale references.

seo.title: JavaScript garbage collection: GC pauses, memory pressure, and debugging jank
seo.description: Learn how JavaScript garbage collection works, why memory pressure creates GC pauses, and how to debug retained references or allocation churn in production.
